question_text: Gel electrophoresis separates DNA fragments based on their:
explanation: Gel electrophoresis separates DNA fragments primarily based on their size (length in base pairs). Since all DNA molecules carry a uniform negative charge per unit length due to the phosphate backbone, the charge-to-mass ratio is essentially the same for all DNA fragments. Therefore, when subjected to an electric field, smaller fragments migrate faster through the gel matrix, while larger fragments migrate more slowly. This size-dependent separation is the fundamental principle of gel electrophoresis as described in NCERT Class 12 Biology.

elimination_hints.hint: DNA fragments are colourless and have no inherent visible shape differences in solution. Gel electrophoresis relies on the sieving effect of the gel on molecules of different sizes, not on colour or shape.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking DNA fragments have visible physical characteristics that can be used for separation.

elimination_hints.hint: While DNA fragments differ in their base composition, gel electrophoresis does not separate them based on this property. The negative charge is contributed by the phosphate backbone, which is the same regardless of the bases present.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing DNA sequencing methods with gel electrophoresis separation principle.

elimination_hints.hint: The type of sugar (ribose vs deoxyribose) distinguishes RNA from DNA but is not the basis for separation in gel electrophoresis. All DNA fragments share the same deoxyribose sugar backbone.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the structural difference between DNA and RNA with the principle of electrophoretic separation.

question_text: In gel electrophoresis, DNA fragments migrate towards the:
explanation: DNA molecules carry a net negative charge due to the phosphate groups in their sugar-phosphate backbone. When placed in an electric field during gel electrophoresis, the negatively charged DNA fragments are attracted towards the anode (positive electrode). This migration towards the positive pole is a fundamental aspect of electrophoresis, as described in NCERT Class 12 Biology Chapter 14.

elimination_hints.hint: The cathode is the negative electrode. Since DNA is negatively charged (due to phosphate groups), it would be repelled by the cathode, not attracted to it. Like charges repel each other.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the direction of migration of negatively charged molecules in an electric field.

elimination_hints.hint: A charged molecule in an electric field moves in one direction only — towards the electrode of opposite charge. DNA cannot migrate towards both electrodes simultaneously.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding that charged molecules migrate directionally in an electric field.

elimination_hints.hint: DNA carries a strong net negative charge and will definitely migrate when placed in an electric field. Electrophoresis specifically exploits this property to separate DNA fragments.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to recognize that charged molecules must move when placed in an electric field.

question_text: The most commonly used gel matrix for separating DNA fragments in molecular biology is:
explanation: Agarose gel is the most commonly used matrix for separating DNA fragments in gel electrophoresis. Agarose is a natural polysaccharide extracted from seaweed that forms a porous gel matrix when dissolved in buffer, heated, and allowed to cool. The pore size of the gel acts as a molecular sieve, allowing smaller DNA fragments to pass through more easily than larger ones. This is the standard method described in NCERT Class 12 Biology for analyzing restriction digestion products and PCR amplicons.

elimination_hints.hint: Starch gel was historically used for protein electrophoresis but is not the standard matrix for DNA separation in modern molecular biology. Agarose provides better resolution and is easier to handle for DNA work.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing older electrophoresis matrices with the modern standard for DNA separation.

elimination_hints.hint: Agar is a mixture of agarose and agaropectin. Pure agarose is preferred because agaropectin contains charged sulfate groups that can interfere with DNA migration and resolution.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing agar (a mixture) with its purified component agarose, which is specifically used for DNA electrophoresis.

elimination_hints.hint: Silica gel is used in chromatography and DNA purification columns, not as a matrix for electrophoretic separation of DNA. It does not form the porous gel structure needed for electrophoresis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing DNA purification materials with electrophoresis matrices.

question_text: The negative charge on DNA that enables its migration during gel electrophoresis is due to:
explanation: DNA carries a negative charge because of the phosphate groups present in its sugar-phosphate backbone. Each nucleotide in the DNA strand contains a phosphate group that is ionised at physiological pH, carrying a negative charge. Since every nucleotide contributes one negatively charged phosphate, the overall charge is proportional to the length of the fragment, giving all DNA fragments a similar charge-to-mass ratio. This uniform negative charge is what drives DNA towards the anode during electrophoresis.

options.text: Phosphate groups in the sugar-phosphate backbone

elimination_hints.hint: Nitrogenous bases (A, T, G, C) are involved in base pairing and are located inside the DNA double helix. They do not carry the negative charge responsible for DNA migration; the charge comes from the backbone.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing the charge of DNA to its nitrogenous bases rather than its phosphate backbone.

elimination_hints.hint: Deoxyribose sugar molecules are neutral components of the DNA backbone. They provide the structural framework but do not carry the negative charge. The charge comes from the phosphate groups linked to these sugars.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the structural role of the sugar with the charge-carrying role of the phosphate in the DNA backbone.

elimination_hints.hint: Hydrogen bonds hold the two complementary strands of DNA together but are electrically neutral interactions. They do not contribute to the net charge of DNA molecules.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing interstrand bonding with the overall charge of the DNA molecule.

question_text: During gel electrophoresis, which of the following DNA fragments will migrate the farthest from the well?
explanation: In gel electrophoresis, smaller DNA fragments migrate faster and farther through the gel matrix compared to larger fragments. The agarose gel acts as a molecular sieve — smaller molecules can navigate through the pores more easily, while larger molecules are impeded. Therefore, a 200 bp fragment, being the smallest among the given options, will travel the greatest distance from the well towards the anode during the same period of electrophoresis.

elimination_hints.hint: A 1000 bp fragment is five times larger than a 200 bp fragment and will face more resistance from the gel pores. It will migrate a shorter distance than the 200 bp fragment in the same time.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking larger fragments travel farther because they carry more total charge.

elimination_hints.hint: A 5000 bp fragment is very large and will be significantly retarded by the agarose gel matrix. It will remain much closer to the well compared to smaller fragments.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding the sieving effect of the gel — larger molecules are impeded more, not less.

elimination_hints.hint: A 10000 bp fragment is the largest option and will migrate the least distance from the well. The gel's pore structure severely restricts the movement of very large DNA molecules.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing total charge (more in larger fragments) with the rate of migration, which depends on the charge-to-mass ratio and the sieving effect.

question_text: Ethidium bromide (EtBr) is used in gel electrophoresis to:
explanation: Ethidium bromide (EtBr) is a fluorescent dye that intercalates (inserts itself) between the stacked base pairs of DNA. When exposed to ultraviolet (UV) light, the EtBr-DNA complex fluoresces bright orange, making the DNA bands visible. Without staining, DNA is colourless and invisible in the gel. EtBr staining followed by UV visualization is the standard method for detecting separated DNA fragments after electrophoresis, as mentioned in NCERT Class 12 Biology.

options.text: Increase the speed of DNA migration
Digest the DNA into smaller fragments
Stain DNA so it can be visualised under UV light
Neutralise the charge on DNA fragments

elimination_hints.hint: EtBr does not increase DNA migration speed. In fact, intercalation of EtBr slightly alters DNA mobility. Its primary purpose is visualization, not separation enhancement.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the role of a staining dye with factors that affect electrophoretic mobility.

elimination_hints.hint: EtBr is a dye, not an enzyme. Restriction endonucleases digest DNA into fragments. EtBr has no catalytic or nuclease activity and cannot cut DNA.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the function of a fluorescent dye with that of restriction enzymes.

elimination_hints.hint: EtBr intercalates between base pairs and does not alter the phosphate backbone charge. DNA retains its negative charge after EtBr staining; the dye simply enables fluorescent detection.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking that a staining dye changes the chemical charge properties of DNA.

question_text: A DNA ladder (molecular weight marker) is used in gel electrophoresis to:
explanation: A DNA ladder or molecular weight marker consists of DNA fragments of known sizes. It is loaded in one lane of the gel alongside the sample DNA. After electrophoresis, the positions of the marker bands are compared with the sample bands to estimate the size of the unknown DNA fragments. This is possible because DNA fragments of the same size migrate the same distance under identical conditions. DNA ladders are essential tools for characterising restriction digestion products and PCR amplicons.

options.text: Provide electrical current for DNA migration
Estimate the size of unknown DNA fragments by comparison
Cut DNA at specific restriction sites

elimination_hints.hint: Electrical current is provided by the power supply connected to the electrophoresis apparatus, not by the DNA ladder. The ladder is simply a set of reference DNA fragments loaded into the gel.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the source of electrical current with a molecular size reference tool.

elimination_hints.hint: Restriction enzymes cut DNA at specific recognition sequences. A DNA ladder is a pre-prepared mixture of DNA fragments of known sizes and has no enzymatic cutting function.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing restriction enzymes with molecular weight markers.

elimination_hints.hint: DNA staining is performed by dyes such as ethidium bromide, not by the DNA ladder. The ladder itself needs to be stained to be visible, just like the sample DNA.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing the function of a staining dye to a size reference marker.

question_text: The process of extracting separated DNA fragments from an agarose gel for further use is called:
explanation: Elution is the process of extracting or recovering separated DNA fragments from the agarose gel after electrophoresis. The desired DNA band is first identified under UV light, cut out of the gel, and then the DNA is extracted from the gel slice using various methods such as electroelution, gel dissolution, or commercial gel extraction kits. The purified DNA can then be used for downstream applications like cloning, ligation, or sequencing. This step is mentioned in NCERT Class 12 Biology as part of recombinant DNA technology procedures.

elimination_hints.hint: Transformation is the process by which bacteria take up foreign DNA from the surrounding environment. It is a method of introducing recombinant DNA into host cells, not a method of extracting DNA from gels.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a DNA uptake method used in genetic engineering with a DNA recovery method from gels.

elimination_hints.hint: Transduction is the transfer of genetic material from one bacterium to another mediated by a bacteriophage (virus). It is unrelated to the recovery of DNA from agarose gels.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up bacteriophage-mediated gene transfer with gel extraction techniques.

elimination_hints.hint: Transfection refers to the introduction of foreign DNA into eukaryotic cells. It is a gene delivery method, not a procedure for extracting DNA bands from an electrophoresis gel.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing eukaryotic gene transfer methods with DNA recovery from gel electrophoresis.

question_text: Polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) is preferred over agarose gel electrophoresis when:
explanation: Polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) has smaller and more uniform pore sizes compared to agarose gels, making it suitable for separating very small DNA fragments (typically less than 500 bp) and proteins with high resolution. Agarose gels are better suited for separating larger DNA fragments (typically 100 bp to 50 kb). PAGE is also the standard technique for separating proteins based on their molecular weight (SDS-PAGE). When high resolution is needed for small molecules, PAGE is the method of choice.

options.text: Very large DNA fragments (above 20 kb) need to be separated
Rapid but low-resolution separation is required
High-resolution separation of small DNA fragments or proteins is needed
DNA needs to be visualised without any staining

elimination_hints.hint: Very large DNA fragments are better separated using agarose gels (or specialised techniques like pulsed-field gel electrophoresis). Polyacrylamide gels have small pores that would not allow large fragments to migrate effectively.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking PAGE is suitable for large DNA fragments when it is actually designed for small molecules.

elimination_hints.hint: PAGE provides high resolution, not low resolution. If rapid, low-resolution separation were needed, standard agarose gel electrophoresis would be more appropriate and simpler to set up.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the resolution capabilities of polyacrylamide and agarose gels.

elimination_hints.hint: Both agarose and polyacrylamide gels require staining (such as with EtBr or silver staining) for DNA visualization. Neither gel type makes DNA inherently visible without a staining step.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking that the choice of gel matrix eliminates the need for DNA staining.

question_text: Southern blotting, which involves transfer of DNA from gel to a membrane, was developed by:
explanation: Southern blotting was developed by Edwin Southern in 1975. In this technique, DNA fragments separated by gel electrophoresis are transferred (blotted) from the agarose gel onto a nitrocellulose or nylon membrane. The DNA on the membrane can then be hybridised with a labelled probe to detect specific sequences. The technique is named after its inventor, and subsequent adaptations for RNA (Northern blotting) and protein (Western blotting) were named by analogy, not after their inventors.

elimination_hints.hint: Frederick Sanger developed DNA sequencing methods (Sanger sequencing / dideoxy method). He did not develop the blotting technique for transferring DNA from gels to membranes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the inventor of DNA sequencing with the inventor of Southern blotting.

elimination_hints.hint: Kary Mullis invented the Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) for DNA amplification. He was not involved in developing blotting techniques for DNA transfer from gels.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up the inventor of PCR with the inventor of Southern blotting.

elimination_hints.hint: Paul Berg is known as the father of genetic engineering for his pioneering work on recombinant DNA. He did not develop the Southern blotting technique.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the pioneer of recombinant DNA technology with the inventor of a DNA detection technique.

question_text: Increasing the concentration of agarose in a gel used for electrophoresis will:
explanation: The concentration of agarose in the gel determines the pore size of the gel matrix. A higher agarose concentration creates smaller pores, which increases the resolution of smaller DNA fragments but makes it difficult for larger fragments to migrate. Conversely, a lower agarose concentration creates larger pores, allowing better separation of larger DNA fragments. This relationship between gel concentration and resolution is an important practical consideration when designing gel electrophoresis experiments.

options.text: Increase the pore size and improve separation of large fragments
Have no effect on the separation of DNA fragments
Cause DNA to migrate towards the cathode instead of the anode
Decrease the pore size and improve resolution of smaller fragments

elimination_hints.hint: Increasing agarose concentration decreases pore size, not increases it. More agarose polymer creates a denser mesh with smaller openings. Larger pore sizes result from lower agarose concentrations.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the relationship between agarose concentration and pore size.

elimination_hints.hint: Gel concentration is a critical parameter that directly affects DNA separation. Changing the concentration significantly alters the sieving properties of the gel and therefore the resolution of different size ranges.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to recognise the importance of gel concentration in determining separation quality.

elimination_hints.hint: The direction of DNA migration is determined by the charge on DNA (negative) and the polarity of the electric field, not by the gel concentration. DNA always migrates towards the anode regardless of agarose concentration.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking that gel properties can change the fundamental direction of charged molecule migration.

question_text: Bromophenol blue is added to DNA samples before loading onto the gel because it:
explanation: Bromophenol blue is a tracking dye included in the loading buffer (loading dye) added to DNA samples before gel electrophoresis. It serves two important purposes: (1) it makes the sample visible so that the researcher can see the sample being loaded into the wells, and (2) it migrates through the gel at a known rate, allowing the researcher to monitor the progress of electrophoresis and know when to stop the run. It does not stain DNA or affect its migration; it simply acts as a visual tracking reference.

options.text: It stains DNA permanently for visualisation under UV light
It digests contaminating RNA in the sample
It serves as a tracking dye to monitor the progress of electrophoresis
It increases the negative charge on DNA for faster migration

elimination_hints.hint: Bromophenol blue does not stain DNA. Ethidium bromide (EtBr) is used for DNA staining and UV visualisation. Bromophenol blue simply migrates alongside DNA as a visible reference front.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the tracking dye (bromophenol blue) with the DNA-staining dye (ethidium bromide).

elimination_hints.hint: Bromophenol blue is a synthetic dye with no enzymatic activity. RNA contamination is removed using RNase, not tracking dyes. Bromophenol blue serves only a visual tracking function.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing enzymatic RNA-degrading activity to a non-enzymatic tracking dye.

elimination_hints.hint: Bromophenol blue does not alter the charge on DNA molecules. DNA's negative charge is inherent to its phosphate backbone. The dye migrates independently in the electric field as a visual indicator.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking a tracking dye changes the electrophoretic properties of DNA.

question_text:
Assertion (A): In gel electrophoresis, all DNA fragments migrate towards the positive electrode (anode).
Reason (R): DNA molecules carry a net negative charge due to the phosphate groups in their sugar-phosphate backbone.

Select the correct option:
(A) Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
(C) A is correct but R is incorrect
(D) A is incorrect but R is correct
explanation: Both the Assertion and the Reason are factually correct. DNA fragments do migrate towards the anode (positive electrode) during gel electrophoresis (Assertion is correct). The Reason correctly identifies that DNA carries a net negative charge because of its phosphate groups. This negative charge is precisely what causes DNA to be attracted towards the positively charged anode in the electric field. Therefore, the Reason is the correct and direct explanation of the Assertion.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason directly explains the Assertion. The negative charge from phosphate groups is the exact cause of DNA migration towards the positive electrode. There is a clear causal link between R and A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to recognise the direct cause-and-effect relationship between DNA's charge and its direction of migration.

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason is factually correct. DNA indeed carries a net negative charge due to its phosphate groups. This is a well-established biochemical fact central to the principle of gel electrophoresis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly doubting the source of negative charge on DNA molecules.

elimination_hints.hint: The Assertion is correct. DNA migration towards the anode is the fundamental basis of gel electrophoresis. This is a universally observed phenomenon in every electrophoresis experiment.
elimination_hints.misconception: Questioning the basic direction of DNA migration in an electric field.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Ethidium bromide is used to visualise DNA bands in a gel because it binds to DNA and fluoresces under visible light.
Reason (R): Ethidium bromide intercalates between the stacked base pairs of DNA.

Select the correct option:
(A) Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
(B) Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
(C) A is correct but R is incorrect
(D) A is incorrect but R is correct
explanation: The Reason is correct — ethidium bromide does intercalate between the stacked base pairs of DNA, which is a well-established fact. However, the Assertion contains an error: EtBr-DNA complexes fluoresce under ultraviolet (UV) light, not visible light. Under visible light, the fluorescence is not detectable. Since the Assertion has a factual inaccuracy (visible light instead of UV light), it is incorrect while the Reason remains correct. Therefore, option D is the correct answer.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The Assertion contains an error: it states that EtBr fluoresces under visible light, but EtBr-DNA complexes actually fluoresce under ultraviolet (UV) light. Since A is incorrect, this option cannot be right.
elimination_hints.misconception: Accepting the incorrect claim about visible light fluorescence without verifying the type of light needed.

elimination_hints.hint: This option assumes both A and R are correct. However, the Assertion is incorrect because EtBr-DNA complexes require UV light, not visible light, for fluorescence. An incorrect statement cannot be evaluated for its relationship with R.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not carefully reading the Assertion to identify the factual error about the type of light.

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason is correct. EtBr is well-known to intercalate between the stacked bases of the DNA double helix, which is the molecular basis for its fluorescent properties when bound to DNA.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the well-established mechanism of ethidium bromide binding to DNA.

question_text:
Match Column I with Column II:

Column I | Column II
A. Ethidium bromide | 1. Transfer of DNA from gel to membrane
B. DNA ladder | 2. Separation of small DNA fragments and proteins
C. Southern blotting | 3. Staining agent for DNA visualisation under UV light
D. PAGE | 4. Molecular weight marker for size estimation

Select the correct matching:
explanation: The correct matching is: A-3 (Ethidium bromide is a fluorescent staining agent that intercalates into DNA and glows under UV light for visualisation), B-4 (DNA ladder is a set of DNA fragments of known sizes used as a molecular weight marker), C-1 (Southern blotting is the technique for transferring DNA from an agarose gel to a nitrocellulose or nylon membrane), D-2 (PAGE or polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis is used for high-resolution separation of small DNA fragments and proteins).

elimination_hints.hint: This option matches EtBr with molecular weight marker (A-4). EtBr is a dye, not a set of DNA fragments. DNA ladder serves as the molecular weight marker, and EtBr is the staining agent.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the function of a staining dye with that of a size reference standard.

elimination_hints.hint: This option matches EtBr with DNA transfer (A-1) and Southern blotting with size estimation (C-4). Southern blotting is the DNA transfer technique, while EtBr is used for staining. These are reversed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the functions of different gel electrophoresis tools and techniques.

elimination_hints.hint: This option matches EtBr with small fragment separation (A-2) and PAGE with size estimation (D-4). PAGE is the gel used for small fragments, while EtBr is a staining dye. Their functions are mixed up.
elimination_hints.misconception: Randomly assigning functions without understanding the specific roles of each tool.

question_text:
Match Column I with Column II:

Column I | Column II
A. Agarose gel | 1. Edwin Southern
B. Elution | 2. Acts as a molecular sieve with pores
C. Southern blotting | 3. Bromophenol blue
D. Tracking dye | 4. Recovery of DNA fragments from the gel

Select the correct matching:
explanation: The correct matching is: A-2 (Agarose gel acts as a molecular sieve whose pores separate DNA fragments by size), B-4 (Elution is the process of recovering or extracting separated DNA fragments from the gel), C-1 (Southern blotting was developed by Edwin Southern for transferring DNA from gel to membrane), D-3 (Bromophenol blue is a commonly used tracking dye that helps monitor the progress of electrophoresis).

elimination_hints.hint: This option matches agarose gel with Edwin Southern (A-1). Edwin Southern developed the blotting technique, not the agarose gel matrix. Agarose gel functions as a molecular sieve.
elimination_hints.misconception: Associating the inventor of a blotting technique with the gel matrix itself.

elimination_hints.hint: This option matches agarose gel with bromophenol blue (A-3). Bromophenol blue is a tracking dye added to samples, not the gel matrix. Agarose gel's key function is acting as a molecular sieve.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the gel matrix with a tracking dye used during loading.

elimination_hints.hint: This option matches agarose gel with DNA recovery (A-4). While DNA is recovered from agarose gels, the process is called elution. The defining property of agarose gel is its sieving function, not DNA recovery.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assigning the elution function to the gel itself rather than recognising it as a separate recovery process.

question_text: Statement: In gel electrophoresis, smaller DNA fragments migrate faster and travel farther through the gel compared to larger fragments.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. The agarose gel acts as a molecular sieve during electrophoresis. Smaller DNA fragments encounter less resistance as they pass through the pores of the gel and therefore migrate faster and farther from the well. Larger fragments are more impeded by the gel matrix and migrate more slowly. This size-dependent separation is the fundamental principle underlying gel electrophoresis, as described in NCERT Class 12 Biology Chapter 14.

elimination_hints.hint: The statement accurately describes the fundamental principle of gel electrophoresis. Smaller fragments do indeed migrate faster due to less friction with the gel pores. This is a core concept in NCERT Class 12 Biology.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the basic size-migration relationship in gel electrophoresis.

elimination_hints.hint: This is a true-false question with only two valid options (True or False).
elimination_hints.misconception: N/A

elimination_hints.hint: This is a true-false question with only two valid options (True or False).
elimination_hints.misconception: N/A

question_text: Statement: In gel electrophoresis, DNA fragments migrate towards the cathode (negative electrode) because DNA carries a positive charge.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. DNA carries a net negative charge (not positive) due to the phosphate groups in its sugar-phosphate backbone. Because of this negative charge, DNA fragments migrate towards the anode (positive electrode), not the cathode (negative electrode). The statement is incorrect on both counts: the charge on DNA and the direction of migration. In gel electrophoresis, negatively charged DNA is attracted to the positively charged anode.

elimination_hints.hint: The statement contains two errors: DNA is negatively charged (not positively charged), and it migrates towards the anode (positive electrode), not the cathode (negative electrode). The statement is clearly false.
elimination_hints.misconception: Accepting incorrect claims about both the charge on DNA and its direction of migration.

elimination_hints.hint: This is a true-false question with only two valid options (True or False).
elimination_hints.misconception: N/A

elimination_hints.hint: This is a true-false question with only two valid options (True or False).
elimination_hints.misconception: N/A

question_text: The separated DNA fragments can be visualised as bright orange bands when stained with ______ and exposed to ultraviolet light.
explanation: Ethidium bromide (EtBr) is the fluorescent dye used to stain DNA in gel electrophoresis. EtBr intercalates between the base pairs of DNA, and when the gel is exposed to ultraviolet (UV) light, the EtBr-DNA complex fluoresces bright orange, allowing the DNA bands to be visualised and photographed. This is the standard method for detecting DNA after gel electrophoresis as described in NCERT Class 12 Biology Chapter 14.

elimination_hints.hint: Bromophenol blue is a tracking dye added to the loading buffer to monitor electrophoresis progress. It does not stain DNA and does not fluoresce under UV light. It serves a completely different function from EtBr.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the tracking dye (bromophenol blue) with the DNA-staining dye (ethidium bromide).

elimination_hints.hint: Crystal violet is used for staining bacteria in the Gram staining procedure in microbiology. It is not used for DNA visualisation in gel electrophoresis under UV light.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a bacteriological stain with a nucleic acid fluorescent dye.

elimination_hints.hint: Methylene blue can stain DNA but does not produce bright fluorescence under UV light like ethidium bromide. EtBr is the standard and most commonly cited dye for UV-based DNA visualisation in NCERT.
elimination_hints.misconception: Selecting a general biological stain instead of the specific fluorescent intercalating dye used in gel electrophoresis.

question_text:
Scenario: A researcher digests a plasmid DNA with a restriction enzyme and runs the products on an agarose gel alongside a DNA ladder. After staining with ethidium bromide and viewing under UV light, she observes three distinct bands in the sample lane at positions corresponding to 3000 bp, 1500 bp, and 500 bp on the DNA ladder.

Question: What can be correctly concluded from this observation?
explanation: The observation of three distinct DNA bands after restriction digestion indicates that the restriction enzyme cut the plasmid at two specific sites, generating three fragments of sizes 3000 bp, 1500 bp, and 500 bp. The total size of the plasmid can be estimated by adding these fragment sizes: 3000 + 1500 + 500 = 5000 bp (5 kb). The number of fragments produced is always one more than the number of cuts in a circular DNA molecule (like a plasmid). This type of analysis is a standard application of gel electrophoresis in molecular biology.

options.text: The restriction enzyme cut the plasmid at three sites, producing three fragments
The 3000 bp fragment migrated the farthest from the well
The restriction enzyme has two recognition sites in the plasmid, and the total plasmid size is approximately 5000 bp
The three bands represent three different plasmids present in the sample

elimination_hints.hint: For a circular DNA like a plasmid, the number of fragments produced equals the number of cuts. Three fragments result from two cuts (two restriction sites), not three. Three cuts would produce three fragments only in linear DNA, but four fragments in circular DNA.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding that circular DNA produces n fragments from n cuts, so 3 fragments come from 2 cuts.

elimination_hints.hint: In gel electrophoresis, smaller fragments migrate farther. The 3000 bp fragment is the largest and would be closest to the well. The 500 bp fragment, being the smallest, would have migrated the farthest.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the relationship between fragment size and migration distance in gel electrophoresis.

elimination_hints.hint: The researcher digested a single plasmid with a restriction enzyme. The three bands represent three fragments generated from cutting one plasmid molecule at two sites, not three separate plasmids.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing restriction digestion fragments from a single DNA molecule with multiple different DNA molecules.
